quotes.verbatim_quote:
This final step is a straightforward process of applying a chord to the first melody note of each bar
and/or chord change in the lead sheet.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Concrete placement rule for the painting-by-numbers method — chord on bar 1 or chord-change beat.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Choose chord voicings that have the same interval of the melody note as the highest note in the
 chord shape.
quotes.why_load_bearing: The single load-bearing voicing-selection rule that the whole method depends on.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
a voicing that can be slightly adapted to have the melody note as the highest note and still sound
good.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Carve-out from the strict rule: voicings can be tweaked rather than always matched exactly.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
To know which voicing to pick we need to establish:

1. The string the melody note is on
2. The type of chord that is in the chord progression whilst that melody note is played
3. The interval of the melody note in relation to that chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the three index keys for the chord-dictionary lookup.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Easily fixed – just shift the shape down on the fretboard so that the top note matches the same fret
where the melody note is on our score:
quotes.why_load_bearing: Operational rule for adapting a dictionary voicing into the key of the tune at hand.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In fact, less is more – placing chords at key places ‘here and there’ is easier on the hands and is
usually more than enough to imply the harmony beneath the melody line. So don’t get carried away!
quotes.why_load_bearing: Pedagogical rule against overharmonization, with the implied-harmony rationale.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
“If it’s easy on the hands, the music will flow better – so most of the time making things as easy
 as possible is musically the best option anyway”.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Cited rule that justifies cutting voicings to improve playability — the key principle behind the refine step.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
We have some options here to make this passage more playable:

1. Cut out notes from the chord – e.g. we could cut that low B to make it easier to change to the
next chord:
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names the first of three remediation strategies for awkward voicings.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
2. Choose a different harmonization approach – e.g. instead of block chords I could use parallel
octaves or diads (a “chord” with only two notes). Here’s an example of diads in 3rds:
quotes.why_load_bearing: Second remediation strategy and definition of diads in the method.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Removing the difficult voicings completely often makes the whole passage work much better on the
guitar – it helps my hands to relax.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Third remediation strategy — author's most-used escape valve for awkward voicings.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The jazzy sound in arrangements usually comes from adding extensions and tensions to voicings –
particularly to dominant chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Rule of thumb: where the 'jazzy' quality lives and which chord type to target for adding tensions.
